# How to build a brand that brings high-ticket buyers to you

**Format.** 60-minute paid teaching session. Business owners. No pitch. Pure value.
**Outcome for the audience.** Walk out with a clear personal brand plan and a $1,000 LinkedIn Profile Optimizer.
**Voice.** Purely Personal. One sentence per paragraph. Plain English. No emojis. No em dashes.
**Promise.** By minute 60 every person in the room knows what to post tomorrow morning.

---

## The 60 minutes

| Block | Time | Slides | Beat |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Open** | 0:00 – 0:05 | 1 – 3 | Hook. Pattern interrupt. Frame the hour. |
| **Story** | 0:05 – 0:15 | 4 – 8 | Broke to stage with Kiyosaki, Mac Attram, Sandy Jadeja. Why brand. |
| **The shift** | 0:15 – 0:23 | 9 – 12 | What AI just did to the price of skill. Why trust is now the moat. |
| **The framework** | 0:23 – 0:43 | 13 – 22 | One platform. One year. One audience. One CTA. |
| **The work** | 0:43 – 0:52 | 23 – 25 | What this looks like Monday morning. |
| **Giveaway** | 0:52 – 0:57 | 26 – 27 | The $1,000 LinkedIn Profile Optimizer. Drop the URL. |
| **Q&A** | 0:57 – 1:00 | 28 | One question. One answer. Off stage. |

---

## Block 1 — Open (5 min)

**Goal.** In the first 60 seconds make every person in the room sit up.

**Slide 1 — Title.**
"How to build a brand that brings high-ticket buyers to you."
Subtitle: "Without becoming louder. Without becoming someone you're not."
Speaker: walk on. Pause. Don't speak for five seconds.

**Slide 2 — The question.**
"Why do some people charge $20,000 for the same hour someone else charges $200?"
Speaker: it's not skill. It's not experience. It's not even results. I'll tell you what it is in the next 55 minutes.

**Slide 3 — The frame.**
"Today is not about getting more clients. Today is about being the person they were already looking for."
Speaker: I'm not selling you anything today. I was paid to be here. So here is what I would charge $25,000 to teach a private client. You get the whole thing in 60 minutes.

---

## Block 2 — Story (10 min)

**Goal.** Earn the right to teach. The story is the proof.

**Slide 4 — Where I started.**
"Broke. No team. No list. No credibility."
Speaker: I'm not from money. I didn't go to a famous school. I was charging $300 to build websites and I couldn't get paid on time.

**Slide 5 — The crossover moment.**
"I stopped chasing clients and started building a brand."
Speaker: I made one decision. I would treat my own name like a company. Not a freelancer. A brand.

**Slide 6 — The names.**
"Robert Kiyosaki. Mac Attram. Sandy Jadeja. Same stage. Same green room."
Bottom tag (red): "2020: broke. 2026: $250,000 from a stage."
Speaker: a year and a half later I was sharing a stage with the people whose books were on my shelf when I was broke. Five years after that, this year, I generated a quarter of a million dollars from a single stage. I want you to write this down. The stage didn't change. I changed.

**Slide 7 — What I sold.**
"I didn't have a better offer. I had a better brand."
Speaker: my work was decent. Not the best. But people in the room had already decided they trusted me before I opened my mouth. That's the asymmetry I want to give you today.

**Slide 8 — The lesson.**
"The brand walks in the room before you do."
Speaker: that's the whole game. By the time a high-ticket buyer is on a call with you, the sale is already 80% closed. Or 80% lost.

---

## Block 3 — The shift (8 min)

**Goal.** Make them feel the urgency of 2026. AI changed the math.

**Slide 9 — What just happened.**
"A skill that used to cost $50,000 a year now costs $20 a month."
Speaker: copywriting. Design. Coding. Research. Editing. Every skill you used to hire for has been compressed into a subscription. This is not a future thing. This happened already.

**Slide 10 — The bundling.**
"Skills are getting bundled. Faster. Cheaper. Better."
Speaker: a single Claude agent now does what a junior team of five did in 2023. Your competitor across the world has the same tools you do. For the same price. Today.

**Slide 11 — The collapse.**
"Anyone can copy anyone. Anyone can sound like anyone."
Speaker: someone can clone your tone in a weekend. They can replicate your offer in an afternoon. Your funnel in an hour. Your content style in ten minutes.

**Slide 12 — The one thing left.**
"Trust is the most expensive asset of 2026."
Speaker: trust is the only thing that does not compress. It is the only asset AI cannot copy. It is the thing buyers pay a 10x premium for. It is what you are actually building when you build a personal brand.

---

## Block 4 — The framework (20 min)

**Goal.** Give them a system they can implement Monday. One platform. One year. One audience. One call to action.

**Slide 13 — The framework header.**
"One. One. One. One."
Speaker: I have built this brand and helped hundreds of founders do the same. Every single one of them that worked followed four ones. Every single one that failed broke at least one of them.

**Slide 14 — One platform.**
"Pick one. Stay there for a year. Do not split your time."
Speaker: most founders fail because they post on five platforms badly instead of one platform well. Pick the platform where your buyer scrolls when they cannot sleep at 11pm. That's the one. For most of you in this room, it is LinkedIn.

**Slide 15 — Why one platform wins.**
"You don't need 100,000 followers. You need the right 1,000."
Speaker: 1,000 right people will pay you more than 100,000 wrong people. One platform lets the algorithm understand you. It lets your audience recognize you. It compounds.

**Slide 16 — One year.**
"You don't have a content problem. You have a quitting problem."
Speaker: nothing compounds in 30 days. Or 90. Or 180. A real personal brand takes a year of showing up before anything looks like it's working. Most founders quit at month four. That's the only reason this works for the ones who don't.

**Slide 17 — The compound math.**
"Months one to three: invisible. Months four to nine: tested. Month ten onward: inbound."
Speaker: I will tell you the truth about timeline. Months one to three nobody reads you. Months four to nine the algorithm starts to figure out who you are. Month ten the messages start coming in. Year two everything changes.

**Slide 18 — One target audience.**
"Stop trying to be useful to everyone. Be unforgettable to one person."
Speaker: write down one person. Their first name. Their job title. Their biggest unspoken fear about their business right now. That is the only person you write for.

**Slide 19 — The mirror test.**
"I write every post like it's a DM to one person. That's why the right one always replies."
Speaker: your content should make one specific person say "this is exactly what I needed to hear today." If everyone is your audience, no one is your audience.

**Slide 20 — One call to action.**
"One ask. Repeated. Quietly. For a year."
Speaker: do not ask for ten things. Do not put five links in your bio. One CTA. Same one. Every post. For 12 months.

**Slide 21 — What a great CTA sounds like.**
"Comment WORD and I'll send you the [thing]."
Speaker: not "DM me to learn more." Not "book a call." A specific word, a specific deliverable, a specific reason for them to raise their hand.

**Slide 22 — Why the four ones work.**
"Repetition is not boring. Repetition is recognition."
Speaker: when buyers see the same person, on the same platform, talking to the same audience, with the same ask, for a year — they don't get bored. They get certain. Certainty is what they pay you for.

---

## Block 5 — The work (9 min)

**Goal.** Make them feel that this is doable starting tomorrow.

**Slide 23 — The Monday morning version.**
"Two hours a week. Done from your phone. No team. No agency."
Speaker: this is not a 40-hour-a-week social media job. I do this in two hours every Monday. I have a system. I'll show you the bones of it in the next two slides.

**Slide 24 — The cadence.**
"Three posts a week. One platform. Same window. Same voice."
Speaker: don't post seven days a week. Don't chase trends. Three posts. Same time of day. Tuesday Wednesday Thursday is enough. If you do this for 12 months I will not be the only one in this room speaking on stages next year.

**Slide 25 — The content stack.**
"Story. Lesson. Asset. Repeat."
Speaker: one post that tells a story from your week. One post that teaches a sharp lesson. One post that gives away something useful. That's the whole content strategy. Forever.

---

## Block 6 — The giveaway (5 min)

**Goal.** Deliver the $1,000 of value you promised. Make the room remember you.

**Slide 26 — The gift.**
"Your LinkedIn profile is the storefront. Most of you are leaving the door locked."
Speaker: your LinkedIn profile is being read right now by people who could change your business. And most of them leave because the profile doesn't tell them who you are or what you do. I built a tool that fixes this in ten minutes. I want every single person in this room to use it tonight.

**Slide 27 — The URL.**
"linkedinprofile.purelypersonal.ai"
Speaker: open it now if you want. It is free. It scores your profile on seven dimensions. It rewrites your headline and your about section in your voice. It gives you the exact words to use. This is something I charge $1,000 for as a one-off audit. Tonight it's yours.

---

## Block 7 — Close (3 min)

**Slide 28 — Last frame.**
"The brand walks in the room before you do. Start building yours tonight."
Speaker: I'm going to take one question. Pick someone in the room with their hand up. Answer. Walk off.

---

## What I leave on stage

- The URL on the slide for 30 seconds while I take the question.
- A QR code in the corner of slide 27.
- The phrase "the brand walks in the room before you do" repeated three times in the talk.

## What I do not do

- I do not pitch the workshop.
- I do not mention the cohort.
- I do not collect emails.
- I do not say "follow me on LinkedIn."

The room paid me to teach. I taught. That is the trade.
